title: Grey Anatomy: Endless Chart
description: Swipe a living heartbeat through a greyscale anatomy chart, dodging surgical hazards while diving into flashing organs to keep your combo alive.

howToPlayJson.title: Grey Anatomy: Endless Chart
howToPlayJson.objective: Survive escalating waves of scalpels, syringes, crash carts, and misfired anatomy labels inside a giant grey body diagram, and post the highest score before three hits end the run.

controls.outcome: The heartbeat snaps to a neighboring body lane to avoid incoming hazards.

controls.input: Swipe up or down at junctions
controls.outcome: The heartbeat shifts between upper and lower anatomy paths for tighter escapes and bonus routes.

controls.input: Pass through a flashing organ gate
controls.outcome: You refresh your combo multiplier, but hazard speed and density spike for a few seconds.

steps: Start in the torso of a giant grey anatomy chart with three connected lanes.
Hazards stream inward in waves: scalpels cut across lanes, syringes thrust straight down paths, and anatomy labels slide in as blockers.
Every few seconds one organ gate flashes, such as heart, lung, or brain.
Crossing the flashing organ gate banks a multiplier boost and extends combo time, but triggers a short 'new episode' surge with denser, nast
Skipping the gate is safer, but your multiplier decays and your score pace drops.
The run ends after three hits, then the score is saved to a simple local leaderboard for fast replay.
howToPlayJson.scoring: Score rises from survival time and near-miss dodges. Each flashing organ gate taken increases the multiplier by 1 up to x8, but missing the next gate starts multiplier decay. Consecutive near-misses during a surge add a
tips: Take organ gates early in calm waves; taking one during a dense wave is how big scores are made.
Scalpels usually sweep sideways, so save vertical dives for syringes and stacked blockers.
If your combo timer is low, a risky organ gate is usually worth more than playing safe.
Short rising beeps can signal an incoming surge, while a bass pulse marks the currently active organ gate.
